Important: Where a material/plant is subject to source list requirements, the order book remains binding.

Anyone who uses a "lift-and-shift" approach here, relying on the order book to maintain control (or who habitually uses it everywhere), is in for a surprise: Suddenly, sources are accessed that were thought to be "muted" in the old system, or the prioritization between quotation and simple procurement works differently than expected. This isn't a system error—it's the new standard logic. But you have to understand it to manage planning effectively.
Interfaces don't forgive "simplification."

This level of detail is also required where S/4HANA extends beyond its own system boundaries. The internal simplification of the data model (e.g., in the business partner approach or in inventory management) often clashes with the reality of the connected subsystems.

Whether it's warehouse management systems (non-SAP WMS) or complex EDI connections to suppliers: the interfaces (IDocs) must "translate" this new logic. An experienced eye for message control and integration points is crucial here. After all, what good is the most sophisticated new planning logic in S/4 if the WMS can't interpret the changed partner/master data logic (business partners/CVI) or the altered inventory and status logic (e.g., inventory qualifications/status transitions) ? After go-live, this doesn't mean a minor error, but rather operational standstill.
Why "generalists" are no longer sufficient.

In the current market phase, many companies are resorting to generalists to fill resource gaps. However, S/4HANA requires specialists who have a deep understanding of process integration – from procurement (MM) to shipping (LE/SD).

It's no longer enough to simply program hundreds of Z-reports to patch process gaps ("back to standard"). The real challenge lies in configuring the new standard to serve the company. You have to understand how the new standard works – and where you need to judiciously expand it to maintain a competitive edge.
In short: Quality in the preparation phase saves on the budget for follow-up work.

The budget for S/4HANA is often primarily allocated to licenses and infrastructure. However, the ROI is determined at the foundation: in the clean migration of logistics processes and the stable integration of interfaces.

Companies would be well advised to invest not only in project management, but also in experts who understand the subtle differences between ECC and S/4HANA at the field level. Those who cut corners here and forgo senior expertise will pay twice as much later – through process disruptions, costly rework, and planning that misses the mark.

entry.title: Email Notifications in Purchase Requisition Flexible Workflow
entry.content:
Email Notifications in Purchase Requisition Flexible Workflow

While processing the purchase requisition workflow, email notifications are triggered at different stages. Certain configurations are required to receive these email notifications. SAP provides standard templates for different stages that need to be copied to configure email notifications.

Email Notification to the Workflow Approver

When a purchase requisition is created, and the workflow is triggered then it will go to the approver based on the workflow configuration in the application "Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions”. During this process,

email notifications can be configured to be sent to the approver.

Prerequisites: The Flexible workflow for purchase requisition has already been configured.

Standard Email Template: SWF_CRT_NOTIFY_RECIPIENTS

Pattern for naming Convention: <YY1>_<SCENARIO_ID>_CRT_<ACTIVITY_NODEID> or <YY1>_< SCENARIO_ID>_CRT_ALL

Purchase Requisition workflow scenario and activity node id list:

Scenario Id

Activity Id

Description

WS02000458 (Overall release of regular purchase requisitions)

19

Release of Purchase Requisition

WS02000458 (Overall release of regular purchase requisitions)

29

Release of Reworkable Purchase Requisition  

WS02000458 (Overall release of regular purchase requisitions)

57

Review Workflow of Purchase Requisition

WS02000458 (Overall release of regular purchase requisitions)

42

Rework Purchase Requisition

WS02000471(item-level release of regular purchase requisitions)

20

Release of Purchase Requisition

WS02000471(item-level release of regular purchase requisitions)

31

Release of Reworkable Purchase Requisition  

WS02000471(item-level release of regular purchase requisitions)

56

Review Workflow of Purchase Requisition

WS02000471(item-level release of regular purchase requisitions)

45

Rework Purchase Requisition

WS02000434 (Overall release of central purchase requisitions)

20

Release of Purchase Requisition

WS02000434 (Overall release of central purchase requisitions)

41

Release of Reworkable Purchase Requisition  

WS02000434 (Overall release of central purchase requisitions)

69

Review Workflow of Purchase Requisition

WS02000434 (Overall release of central purchase requisitions)

56

Rework Purchase Requisition

WS02000438 (Item-level release of central purchase requisitions)

32

Release of Purchase Requisition

WS02000438 (Item- level release of central purchase requisitions)

51

Release of Reworkable Purchase Requisition  

WS02000438 (Item- level release of central purchase requisitions)

73

Review Workflow of Purchase Requisition

WS02000438 (Item- level release of central purchase requisitions)

63

Rework Purchase Requisition

 

Steps to be followed:

  1. Open standard email template in “Maintain email templates” application.

 

  1. Select standard email template and click on the Copy button to create custom email template.

 

Name of the Email Template should be given as per the pattern.

Example 1: If you want to create template for specific activity such as “Release of Purchase Requisition” then in this case custom email template name will be YY1_02000458_CRT_19.

 

Example 2: If you want to create template for complete workflow scenario then in this case custom template name will be YY1_02000458_CRT_ALL.  

 

 

 

Email Notification after Workflow Approval or Rejection

When a purchase requisition workflow is approved or rejected by the workflow approver, you can configure email notifications to send emails to notify about the status.

There are two ways to configure email notification to be sent after approval or rejection.

  1. Using email template SWF_WORKFLOW_COMPLETE_NOTIFY: -Using this template, email will be triggered to purchase requisition creator on the approval/rejection.

         Prerequisites: The flexible workflow has already been configured.

         Steps to be followed: -

  1. Open standard email template SWF_WORKFLOW_COMPLETE_NOTIFY in “Maintain email templates” application. 

 

  1. Select standard email template and click on Copy button to create custom email template by given name as per the pattern:  

YY1_<SCENARIO_ID>_COMPLETE_POSITIVE - When workflow approved

YY1_<SCENARIO_ID>_COMPLETE_NEGATIVE - When workflow rejected.

 

Purchase Requisition workflow approved scenario: YY1_02000458_COMPLETE_POSITIVE

 

 

Purchase Requisition workflow rejection scenario: YY1_00800238_COMPLETE_NEGATIVE

 

 

 

 Notes:

  • If you use the email template SWF_WORKFLOW_COMPLETE_NOTIFY to send email after approval or rejection mentioned above, then email will always go to purchase requisition creator.
  • This is a generic way of configuring emails, so while creating a custom email template, the available data fields will not be specific to the purchase Requisition.
  1. Configure email notification in “Manage workflows for purchase requisitions” application: - 

Using this approach, you will have option to maintain recipient to whom you wanted to send email notification after purchase Requisition workflow approval or rejection and while creating custom email template, available data fields will be more specific to purchase Requisition.

Below standard email templates will be used to create custom email template.

MMPUR_PRITM_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Item Approval

MMPUR_PRITM_WFL_REJECT_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Item Rejection

MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Header Approval

MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_REJECT_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Header Rejection

MMPUR_CPRITM_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Item Approval

MMPUR_CPRITM_WFL_REJECT_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Item Rejection

MMPUR_CPRHDR_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Header Approval

MMPUR_CPRHDR_WFL_REJECT_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Header Rejection

Steps to be followed for creating email templates: -

  1. Open standard email template in “Maintain email templates” application.

Example: header level approval of PR: MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL

 

  1. Select standard email template and click on the Copy button to create custom email. You can take any name for custom form template which does not exist.
  2. Naming Pattern: YY1_<any name>

For approval:

 

 

 

For rejection:

 

 

Deadline Email Notification:

The "Manage Workflows for Purchase Requisitions" Fiori application allows you to configure deadline monitoring and set up custom email notifications to keep approvers informed about pending approvals and new work items, ensuring timely approvals and efficient workflow management.

Copy the pre-delivered email template into the custom name space. The pre-delivered templates for the various workflow scenarios are as follows:

MMPUR_CPRHD_WF_DEADLINE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Deadline

MMPUR_CPRITM_WFL_DEADLINE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Central Purchase Requisition Item Deadline

MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_DEADLINE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Header Deadline

MMPUR_PRITM_WFL_DEADLINE_EMAIL - Email Notification for Purchase Requisition Item Deadline

custom template must follow the following pattern: YY1_<Any Name>

For example: YY1_MyNotificationTemplate

 

 

Transport email template:

Open app “Configure Software Package”, click Add Registration, and then select any package

 

 

Active “automatic task handling”

 

Create a workbench TR by using SE10

 

 

 

For TR to package by using “Configure Software Package”

 

 

Assign Template to package by using app “Register Extensions for transport”

Select Templates and reassign package.

 

 

For a different landscape (DEV to QA), release the transport request and import it into the target client.

Configure notification while creating workflow:

  1. Open “Manage workflows for purchase Requisitions” application to create workflow. 
  2. To create workflow, click on “Create” button

 

 

 

  1. Go to the Notification tab and click on the create button.

 

  1. Fill in the required details and click on the create button.

Workflow Result: To enable notifications after approval or rejection, select the appropriate option from the dropdown menu.

E-Mail Template: For all the workflow result action, all the configured email template for the overall purchase Requisition approval and rejection will be displayed, which are created by copying pre-delivered email templates: -

MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_APPROVE_EMAIL for approval

MMPUR_PRHDR_WFL_REJECT_EMAIL for Rejection

 

Please select proper email template for each action.

 

Recipients: Under recipients section you can maintain user to whom you want to send email notification after approval/rejection using fields Assignment By and Role.

For example, if you want to send email notification to workflow initiator then you can select value for “Assignment By” as “Role” and value for Role as “Workflow initiator”.

Configured details for approval: Fill below details and Click on Create button. After clicking on Create button you will be able to see email notification configured under notification tab.

 

To configure email notification in same workflow for rejection email click on “Create” button again under notification tab.

 

Fill required details and click on “Create” button.

Email notification will show for both approval and rejection under “Notification” tab.

 

For more details, refer to How to Configure the Flexible Workflow for Purchase Requisitions

entry.title: Violent Geometry of Gold: from Flatlandia to Spacelandia
entry.content:
Let us talk gold.  And Newmont Corp:

Newmont Corporation (stock ticker NEM) is the world’s largest gold miner.  And one of the top SAP customers.  Same is true for Barrick Mining (B), its competitor and – in certain areas – a partner.  For example, Barrick-Newmont JV in Nevada is the world’s largest gold complex.  Barrick is also an SAP customer.

Just to put things in perspective, here is the heat map of the EV for the various players in metals and mining:

 

WHY GOLD RETURNS?  AND PAIRING GOLD-STOCK RETURNS

Recent gold's ascension to unprecedented value prompted many to examine its return dynamics and correlations with various other returns.  We are decided to follow suit.

First, we define returns: stock price changes in NEM and B vs price changes in gold.  Specifically, the raw price data allows us to create time series of returns: first for Newmont Corporation’s stock and the second for gold.  In both cases, monthly returns are defined as

 

 

 

Dataset: we took 1985-2025 (40 years) gold prices from the World Gold Council (gold.org) and Newmont stock prices (NEM) from Yahoo Finance, turning both into returns.  The resulting data contains 490 monthly returns.

datenem_returngold_return
1985-01-01  
1985-02-01(0.037)(0.011)
1985-03-010.0380.073
1985-04-010.000(0.019)
1985-05-010.0200.020
1985-06-01(0.075)(0.015)
1985-07-010.0720.006
1985-08-010.0110.001
1985-09-01(0.069)0.016
1985-10-010.0590.081
1985-11-010.0760.109
1985-12-01(0.018)0.014
1986-01-010.077(0.061)

And most recent ones:

Let us zoom in on the first pair of returns: 1985 February saw NEM stock price decline by 0.037 and gold decline by 0.011, i.e., the two monthly returns were -0.011 and -0.037 respectively.

Pairing these returns essentially answers the following question: “Given gold moved by this amount in month t, how did Newmont move in the same month?”

  • Statistics call this a bivariate observation (or observation from a joint distribution)
  • Finance calls this a paired return realization (or joint asset-factor return where gold is a factor)
  • Mathematically, such paired return is point or vector in, i.e., in two-dimensional space. We will prefer the vector nomenclature here.

With the vector of returns definition in mind, we have the following for February 1985:

 

 

Plotting all such paired returns produces the following scatterplot:

 

As we established, each dot is a vector of returns.  Visually and intuitively, the vector is an arrow starting at the origin [0,0] and extending to such observation coordinates

as shown on the plot below:

 

Put plainly, in February of 1985, both assets - gold and NEM – were down, i.e., produced negative returns.  This is why the vector resides in the 3rd quadrant.

What is the magnitude, i.e., length of this vector?

This vector magnitude conveys the length of that month’s movement, i.e., how large the combined movement was across both gold and Newmont in February 1985.  How “violent” the month was.

Close up on this vector allows us to see it in greater detail:

 

Specifically, we can visualize the movement: Newmont moved much more down than gold moved left.  This specifically means: both moves into the “negative territory” but Newmont moved much more. 

We estimate the angle in two ways.

  1. Recalling SOHCAHTOA,

            Since we start from the origin and go counter clock-wise to the red vector, the angle is

 

Put another way, measuring θ from the positive x-axis, we have tan(θ) = y / x, which captures relative sensitivity of NEM to gold for that month (for that realization only).  This is local sensitivity, not structural beta (yet).

We use -180 degrees to match the polar coordinates. 

  1. Alternatively, using atan2(y, x), the angle naturally places the vector in Quadrant III, confirming a joint negative regime.

Our economic interpretation is simple: the move happened in quadrant III, i.e., negative economic regime (decline for both) and we know that NEM realized over 3x of the gold negative return.

We contrast February 1985 with March, where both Newmont stock and gold went up producing positive returns:

 

Here, the gold went up 2x of NEM. In other words, gold rallied strongly, NEM participated but more conservatively. Therefore,

Angle of 45 degrees would indicate equal movement, but our angle here is only 27.5 degrees which again indicates x-dominated change as opposed to the y-dominated one.  That month, gold won.

In relative magnitude terms.

But what is important for our inference about the relationship is how much gold’s movement that month “showed up” in NEM’s return?  Answer: approximately 50%.  This is where the interpretation becomes interesting since we observe realization of sensitivity.  We are not estimating NEM’s beta yet; but are observing the sensitivity – via vectors.  In other words, we already see some asymmetry: when gold moved down in February, NEM moved down 3x.  When next month gold moved up, NEM moved 0.5x.

So, we can reformulate our findings so far in this way:

We can start generalizing now.

 

HOW MUCH OF NEM’s MOVEMENT IS EXPLAINED BY GOLD MOVEMENT? 

This question can be reformulated in “vector terms”: what is the projection of the joint NEM/gold return vector onto the gold direction?

We already established that for any given month in our dataset,

Therefore, the gold direction vector is

 

This is a unit vector representing no NEM component.

Now we want to know how much our joint return vector – the red arrow – projects onto gold. In this particular situation, the case is trivial because the gold resides on x axis and the coordinate 0.073 easily depicts such projection.  However, we want to generalize this rule for the more complicated cases where the factor (e.g., gold) direction vector is not easily located on x-axis.  The projection

 

Where the dot product of this projection is visually intuitive here: hypothenuse * cos (adjacent angle theta) = adjacent side (x coordinate): 

 

 

Note: full dot product identity is

We can verify the 0.073 projection by calculating the dot product:

(r * g) = (0.073, 0.038) * (1, 0) = 0.073 * 1 + 0.038 * 0 = 0.073

Alternatively,

In plain English, the red vector points 0.073 in the gold direction, or the gold-aligned component has magnitude of 0.073.  Financially, we can say that in March 1985, 0.073 of the total movement is explained by gold (that month).

We can now reverse the logic of the dot product definition, or, put another way, we can create a second question:

 

Our first question

 --> Our second question

How much r (i.e., total magnitude of this month’s combined change) projects onto gold?

What fraction of the total vector’s length, i.e., ||r|| lies in the gold direction?

Hypothenuse x cos(theta) = adjacent size:

 

 

 

 

 

Scalar – in this case length along x axis

Ratio inside the triangle =

 

How many percentage points of this month’s NEM+gold move lie along gold.

Fraction of the joint vector aligned with gold.  Explanatory share. Early “predecessor” of R2 in OLS.

Since we asked the question how much the joint movement is gold-aligned, we ought to investigate the other part as well:

 

WHAT HAPPENS TO THE PART WHICH IS NOT GOLD-ALIGNED? 

Financially, this question centers on non-gold, i.e., NEM-idiosyncratic returns (movements in price).  These movements are uncorrelated with the gold movements.  Well, “uncorrelated” applies to the entire data set, so for one month, the lack of relationship here is better explained as orthogonal to the gold direction by construction (uncorrelated only in aggregate).  We define orthogonality month by month; we discover correlation across the entire period.

Let us again use March 1985 since we are familiar with this month’s returns.  Mathematically, we express the “gold unrelated” NEM movement (return) as orthogonal vector.  In plain English, this is the “leftover” which has zero alignment with gold:

In vector form, this orthogonal vector (perpendicular to the gold projection) is

i.e., difference between two vectors: joint movement vector – its projection onto gold.  Why is this vector perpendicular to the “gold vector”?  Because we made gold our explanatory variable.  If we were to use the typical regression language, gold return[s] is the independent (predictor) variables while NEM’s return[s] is the dependent (predicted) variable.  Clearly, the relationship in reality may be bi-directional and more nuanced, but for now we made “an executive decision”, so to speak: treat gold as the explanatory and NEM as the explained variable (x and y respectively).  This is why the orthogonal vector (blue arrow) is perpendicular to the “gold vector.”

 

Financially,

  • the dotted line vector along the x axis (the “gold vector”) says, “What did NEM return because of the gold movement this month?” (assuming linearity, not causality, and no stability over time)
  • while the vertical blue vector says, “What did NEM return that cannot be explained by gold this month?”

Given this distinction, we can treat the orthogonal (blue) vector as a residual: it provides the gold-unexplained return on the NEM stock. In other words, the projection of the joint vector onto gold represents the gold-explained component of NEM, while the blue vector depicts the gold-unexplained portion of the NEM return.

Now that we defined the joint return vector as

we can plot the gold returns on the x-axis and such residuals on the y-axis:

 

 

Geometry

Mathematics

Statistic

Finance

 

We can see that the residual vectors reside in the direction perpendicular to gold.  We do not see any systematic tilt left: the cloud of the returns is centered on y=0 and appears symmetric.  In other words, we observe typical orthogonality in

 

 

This is the “geometric heart” of the OLS regression.

 

From construction:

 

By definition of projection (our earlier dot product definition):

 

 

Fitted line is approximately flat, so correlation is

corr(Gold,rt,⊥​)≈−0.016

 

 

We estimate beta as:

 

 

This means that when gold moves by 1 unit, NEM moves by 0.075 units.  In other words, +1% gold month corresponds, on average, to +0.075% month for NEM price change. NB! this beta reflects monthly decimal returns and should not be compared directly to conventional annualized betas.

 

Residual:

 

 

 

 

Beta of gold is the systematic gold-driven component of NEM (linear approximation).  This is important for the decision to hedge, increase or decrease gold exposure (via NEM stock), and alleviate macroeconomic shocks.

 

The residual is everything else specific to NEM: mine issues, management decisions and execution, even market noise (equity moves unrelated to gold).

In sum: this plot shows that once we remove the gold-aligned component of NEM’s returns, what remains is geometrically orthogonal, statistically uncorrelated, and economically idiosyncratic.

 

MANY VECTORS: WHICH ONE BEST DESCRIBES THEM ALL?

 

 

As we established earlier, dot product of two vectors measures how much one vector moves along the other, like joint vector movement along gold we described earlier.  Given we have many joint returns (490 precisely), i.e., vectors v1, v2, …, vn (depicted in different colors above), how can we find a single direction vector u such that vectors have large projections onto u?  Let us define “large” first.

If we sum just the projections, some resulting scalars will have negative sign and some positive, so some positive and negative months would cancel.  We would get “net drift” but this is a different story.  So, what do we do?

Like we do in estimate variance, we square the projections:

Therefore, we are looking for the unit vector u which maximizes such sum of squares, or “squared projection”:

 

With the NEM/Gold example, this algorithm chooses a joint Gold–NEM movement direction that explains as much total magnitude of monthly co-movement as possible, regardless of the sign.

Here is the resulting projection.  Given the maximization of the “squared projection” algorithm above, this line has the following meaning: when we orthogonally project all monthly vectors onto this line, the total squared length of those projections is maximized.

 

 

·     Line is almost vertical, which means that NEM returns dominate

·     This is a line, meaning direction +/-u are equivalent: bulls and crashes equally matter

·     Interestingly, our data is clustered around the x axis and is horizontally distributed… but clustering near the x-axis means large directional alignment with the x-axis, but not necessarily large magnitude-weighted projections: even modest-angle but high-magnitude vertical component yt dominates the squared length (magnitude is quadratic).

In other words, many small calm months near horizontal “yield to” fewer crisis / equity-shock months with large vertical magnitude.

·     Gold moves often, but modestly

·     NEM moves less often, but violently

·     Our algorithm rewards violent months quadratically, i.e., though high energy of such months

These findings may feel counter-intuitive at first, therefore we ought to explore it with the vector magnitudes in mind.

We recall that each joint movement vector (paired return vector which we started with) has the following length, or magnitude:

But we remember that our maximization algorithm squares such vector:

In plain English – perhaps not entire correctly but intuitively – vector length is the hypothenuse.  Its projection (the adjacent side) is hypothenuse multiplied by cos(angle) – this comes from the Pythagorean identity.  But the maximization algorithm, squares such projection (the dot product of v and u above).  The hypotenuse measures how large each vector is but contains no information about orientation, so it cannot distinguish between directions; projections introduce directional dependence, and squaring them yields a rotation-sensitive, non-canceling measure of how much joint movement lies along a candidate direction.

 

Panel 1 on the top plots each vector (i.e., joint movement as a vector from the origin). Most months live in a tight band near zero, but a few months spike dramatically (1987, 1998, 2008, 2015, 2020).

Pane 2 in the middle conveys the energy. Since monthly returns are max 0.77 for NEM and 0.18 for gold, squaring them makes them smaller. But the relative importance of such violent months rises.

Panel 3 conveys that clearly: out of 490 months (40 years of data), just 12 (!) have over a quarter of energy.

Our geometric findings map to the financial interpretation:

  • Most months: small, noisy co-movement between gold and NEM
  • Rare months: systemic stress or regime shifts
    • 1987 crash
    • 1998 LTCM / Russia default
    • 2008 GFC
    • 2020 COVID
  • Those months define the economic meaning of “joint movement”

What surprises me here: though most months cluster near the horizontal axis, a few high-energy months dominate the squared-projection algorithm; consequently, the optimal direction aligns with those violent joint movements rather than with the calm majority.

So, we might have proven that violent / high energy months translate into the near vertical orientation of the optimized line, the line that represents single direction of all 490 vectors.  What does it mean?  It does not mean that gold returns are irrelevant or NEM returns are completely independent.  Rather, we can conclude that the dominant joint-movement direction in 1985-2025 is driven by NEM’s variability, not by gold’s average alignment.

The economically meaningful joint shocks between gold and Newmont are equity-driven, not metal-price-driven.

 

CROSS-PRODUCT: WHAT CAN IT TELL US ABOUT NEM AND GOLD?

We will be quickly moving from the ‘Flatlandia’ (to paraphrase Edwin Abbott) into ‘Spacelandia’ or into

Let us pick two months: February and March of 1985.  We remember that the joint movement (or joint return) vectors had the following coordinates (see table above):

 

 

Their cross product is defined as

 

We can see this cross product as the 3rd axis vector sticking up, i.e., perpendicular to the plane of the two original vectors. Small red vector here:

 

 

This red upward looking vector is small because we multiply numbers which are each below |1|.  Interestingly, the magnitude of this vector represents the area of the parallelogram formed by the two original vectors,

 

 

 

To check ourselves, let us calculate the area of this parallelogram “by hand”:

  1. Let us find the length of each vector:

    •  

    •  

  2. Now, let us use the green vector as the base. Area of parallelogram is given by the A=base * height where height = slanted side * sin(angle between slanted and base).
    • To find the sine of the angle we use the dot product and then the relationship between the dot product, magnitude, and angle.
    • Dot product is

       

    •  

  1.  

I find it amazing that this barely visible “z-axis” orthogonal vector – the cross product of our two Feb and March vectors – is indeed the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors.

What does the cross-product vector – or the parallelogram area – mean for NEM and gold?  The magnitude of the orthogonal vector (or area of the parallelogram) represents the joint “volatility” volume, or the degree of uncorrelated movement between gold and NEM.  Well, more correctly, the area measures directional misalignment x magnitude across two realizations.

The larger the area of such parallelogram (or the height of the orthogonal vector), the more NEM and gold moved freely independently, and the smaller the area, the more they acted “in sync”.  If gold and NEM were perfectly “correlated” (this is not correlation yet – which requires many observations) during these two months, the parallelogram would collapse into a single line and the orthogonal vector would be zero in length.  Therefore, in such a scenario, a portfolio of two assets – NEM and gold – would offer no diversification benefit for those realizations.

On the other hand, ideal diversification scenario for these two realizations would be the maximum parallelogram area, where

and the orthogonal vector is maximal in length (magnitude).

 

As a recap for our two asset / two vector situation:

  • Two joint-return vectors (Month 1 and Month 2) form a parallelogram
  • Its area equals the magnitude of the cross product
  • Zero area ⇔ perfect directional alignment
  • Area measures directional change + scale (not correlation in statistics)

Now we can ask ourselves about multiple month pairs within the entire 1985-2025 time horizon: what may the cross product of such pairs tell us? 

 

There is no cross product of the entire period, but we can calculate multiple cross products of month pairs and plot the distribution of such parallelogram areas (aka cross product orthogonal vectors).

 

As a reminder: the smaller the area, the more in sync gold and NEM moved (more colinear) and the larger the area, the more different the returns were: regime break (exogenous events perhaps) and strong directional disagreement / magnitude mismatch.

 

 

 

Rolling 2 adjacent months, i.e., 489 2-month pairs (t, t+1)

All possible month pairs, i.e., 119,805 pairs

How do co-movements (joint returns) for gold and NEM change month to month?

Global cloud geometry

 

 

We see how right skewed data is. Again, many calm months witness relatively “synchronized” movements of gold and NEM returns (left handside).  But there are “violent” months where returns are very “uncoordinated” both in direction (angular disagreement) and magnitude.  In sum, the right handside area of this boxplot represent macroeconomic events and market stress.

This full-pair geometry reinforces the same idea, just with many more pairs.

Calm months dominate in count, violent months dominate in impact. 

Consistent with our earlier findings.

 

 

3D “SPACELANDIA” WITH A 3RD ASSET: WHAT CAN WE LEARN?

First, let us add another company: Barrick Mining.

With this addition, our raw data of returns has one more column:

 

Let us now visualize the 3D space of December 2025:

 

 

 

Here i, j, k denote the standard unit vectors along the Gold (x-), NEM (y-), and Barrick (z-) axes, respectively.

 

 

This single 3D joint-movement vector represents one realization (joint movement returns) in Gold–NEM–Barrick space.
To examine diversification and independence across the three assets, we zoom in on how such vectors span three-dimensional space across time.

While in Flatlandia (2D) the cross product of two vectors - two joint return vectors for Gold and NEM – produced the area of parallelogram (how coordinated or uncoordinated return moves), in Spacelandia (3D), the triple scalar product of three vectors produces the volume of a parallelepiped:

 

 

Thus, this means that now we are looking at three months, e.g., December, November, and October 2025.

In other words,

 

and so on.

 

What does the formula mean geometrically?

​ is vector perpendicular to the plane spanned by

 

  • Its magnitude

  •  

     is the area of the parallelogram built on

  •  

  • Then

  •  

     takes the component of

  •  

    ​ in the perpendicular direction, i.e., “height”
  • So, volume = base area × height.

 

 

 

 

What does “big vs small volume” mean financially?  How many independent directions did the system move across those 3 months.

  • Volume ≈ 0: the 3 monthly joint-movement vectors are nearly coplanar / linearly dependent.
    Across these three months, the Gold–NEM–Barrick system mostly moved in one common pattern; nothing ‘new’ happened in the 3-asset relationship.
  • Large volume: the three months span a genuinely 3D chunk of space.
    Across these three months, the relationship among Gold, NEM, and Barrick produced a new linearly independent shock pattern—these assets did not just scale up/down together; their co-movement structure shifted.

Like we did in the Flatlandia with the cross products across the entire time period (490 months – 1 because of rolling adjacent 2-month convention), for Spacelandia, we can perform the same for the rolling adjacent 3 month parallelepiped volumes:

 

 

We observe somewhat similar picture: there are a lot of calm months where the volume of the RD parallelepiped is small (joint return movements appear synchronized in direction and magnitude) and there are “violent” months where such joint return movements are misaligned in direction and magnitude (volume of the resulting parallelepiped is high).

 

Finance Color #1: It clicked: diversification is episodic, not average

Our geometry shows that diversification between gold and mining equities (NEM and B) is state-dependent, not constant:

  • Most months: small areas / volumes → assets behave as if driven by a common macro regime
  • Rare months: large areas / volumes → diversification suddenly becomes real

Therefore,

  • long-run correlations look “moderate”
  • but portfolio protection often shows up only during stress

In other words, this geometry captures when diversification matters, not just whether it exists on average.

Finance Color #2: Volumes identify regime transitions, not just volatility

This is the “value add” by geometry:

  • Large magnitudes = volatility
  • Large areas = change in pairwise structure
  • Large volumes = change in system structure

So, a large rolling 3-month volume is not “just a volatile period” — it is a period in which the relationship among Gold, Newmont, and Barrick itself changed.  Financial models break down, hedging become unstable, new factors emerge and/or replace previous ones.

As an extra step, I re-examined the history of various events “surrounding” such extreme returns on the right handside.  Picking just a few of those located on the most far end of the x-axis, yields the following picture and details below:

Rare large-volume rolling 3-month windows (chronological)

1986-04 → 1986-06

  • Macro / markets: Post-Volcker disinflation environment (CPI in us at 10-14% so Feds increased the rate beyond 20%); USD strength and falling inflation expectations.
  • Gold: Weak and unstable price action as real rates rose.
  • Miners: Equity-specific adjustments amplified moves relative to gold.
  • Why volume spikes: Gold and miners responded differently to shifting rate expectations, breaking the usual “gold proxy” behavior.

1987-08 → 1987-10

  • Macro / markets: Lead-up to and aftermath of the October 1987 stock market crash (on Oct 19, 1987 Dow Jones lost 22%).
  • Gold: Initially mixed, then benefited as a crisis hedge.
  • Miners: Extreme equity volatility dominated fundamentals.
  • Why volume spikes: Equity shock introduced a new independent direction orthogonal to gold’s behavior.  Liquidity “evaporated” so the correlation is almost 1.

1998-08 → 1998-10

  • Macro / markets: Russian default (domestic debt and subsequent ruble devaluation) + Long-Term Capital Management crisis (ironically, LTCM, a hedge fund, was founded by Nobel Prize laureates in Economics including Robert Merton and Myron Scholes); severe global risk aversion.
  • Gold: Safe-haven demand surged after prolonged weakness.
  • Miners: Sharp equity dislocations due to leverage and funding stress.
  • Why volume spikes: Breakdown of historical gold–miner co-movement during systemic stress.

1999-09 → 1999-11

  • Macro / markets: Late-cycle equity exuberance (NASDAQ is in vertical ascent) approaching dot-com peak.  Traditional valuations? Dismissed.
  • Gold: Structural weakness, including sales (not purchases!) by Central banks.
  • Miners: Equity market forces overwhelmed commodity linkage.  They traded like ordinary stocks rather than gold-linked assets.
  • Why volume spikes: Miners decouple from gold amid speculative equity flows.

2008-09 → 2008-11/12 → 2009-02

  • Macro / markets: Systemic collapse after Lehman, followed by rapid stabilization via extraordinary monetary intervention.
  • Gold: Immediate safe-haven surge during panic, then sustained strength as QE and monetary debasement expectations formed.
  • Miners: Initial collapse with equities despite rising gold, followed by a violent rebound once equity markets stabilized.
  • Why volume spikes: Two-phase regime shift — first forced deleveraging overrides gold linkage, then re-alignment as the system transitions into a new equilibrium.

2016-01 → 2016-03 — Global growth scare → commodity & gold rebound

  • Macro / markets: Early-2016 global risk-off driven by an oil collapse (oil drops to some $26/barrel – lowest in a decade), China growth fears, and recession concerns; sentiment stabilized by March.  Feds: in late 2015 raise rates to 0.5% first time since 2006 (akin to “emergency policy is about to end”)
  • Gold: Strong rally as investors sought safety and repriced lower real rates.
  • Miners: Sharp rebound as gold surged and balance-sheet fears eased.  Both NEM and B enter 2016 deeply out of favor. Had spent years cutting costs, selling assets, and repairing balance sheets.
  • Why volume spikes: Sudden regime shift — from deflation panic to reflation relief — forced rapid repositioning in gold and mining equities.

These simple bullet points provide just some color to how high can the volumes go.  As a reminder: the volumes here (triple scalar product) represent how different the returns were.  Large volumes - the outliers we discuss – indicate situations where gold returns continued but NEM and B returns changed drastically; key is that these returns moved differently relative to each other.

 

RECAP: GEOMETRY = FRIEND OR FOE?

In this note we examined the relationship between gold and Newmont returns through a geometric lens, gradually expanding the dimensionality of analysis.  Then we added Barrick returns and therefore examined even more nuanced interplay of gold and equities as well as time (regimes such as calm months vs. economic and market stress).

First came monthly gold–Newmont returns as joint movement vectors in two-dimensional space.  Vector projections allowed decomposing each month’s movement into a gold-aligned component and an orthogonal component.  This orthogonal residual is geometrically perpendicular to the gold direction by construction, statistically uncorrelated in aggregate, and economically interpretable as NEM-specific behavior (e.g., disposition of a mine, acquisition, or any other NEM-specific and gold-agnostic corporate and market factor).  This construction reveals the geometric foundation of linear regression without invoking probabilistic assumptions.

Moving beyond individual months: many joint-return vectors simultaneously allow – via the maximized squared projections direction - showing that a small number of high-energy months dominate the long-run joint behavior of gold and Newmont (joint return-wise).  Calm months are numerous but economically secondary; crisis periods define the dominant co-movement structure.

The cross product starts bridging Flatlandia to Spacelandia allowing to examine how joint return behavior changes across time. The area of the parallelogram formed by two monthly joint-movement vectors - or the resulting orthogonal vector magnitude - captures directional misalignment and scale across realizations.  Rolling and global distributions of these areas reveal a strongly right-skewed structure: most months are synchronized, while rare episodes reflect regime breaks and stress.  Calm months dominate in count; violent months dominate in impact.  In a sense, the newly formed “z-axis” cross-product confirms the findings of the [purely 2D] squared projections.

Adding Barrick Mining price moves turns this into the 3D “Gold–Newmont–Barrick space.”  Here, diversification and independence are no longer captured by areas but by volumes, measured via the scalar triple product of three joint-movement vectors.  Rolling three-month volumes quantify whether the system behaves as effectively two-dimensional or explores genuinely three-dimensional dynamics.  Periods of large volume correspond to shifts in the joint structure of returns rather than simple increases in volatility.  Our findings here are consistent with those above (projections and cross-products):

The economic meaning of gold–mining equity (NEM an B) relationships is defined not by average behavior, but by rare, high-energy episodes in which co-movement geometry changes.  Vector geometry provides a unified language to describe projection, residuals, regime shifts, and diversification in a way that is intuitive, rigorous, and directly interpretable for financial decision-making.

Perhaps this picture for the 24 years of the market from Visual Capitalist (link) provides an independent validation for our findings:

 

 

ANOTHER RECAP: “SO WHAT” REGARDING THE VECTORS AS ANALYTICAL TOOLS?

Throughout the analysis, it was becoming clearer to me that, on one hand, findings from the previous layer of complexity – e.g., projections and simple orthogonal vector – repeated with the next level of complexity.  So, the question becomes, ‘Why go to another level of complexity then?”  On the other hand, certain additional insights emerge with the growing complexity and increasing dimensionality of the analyses (from ‘Flatlandia’ to ‘Spacelandia’).

This table attempts to summarize the incremental value of the growing dimensionality: from “simple 2D” to “transitional 2D” (cross product and resulting orthogonal vector) to the “full 3D”:

 

Layer / Tool

Dimensional Regime

Mathematical Object

What It Measures (Plain English)

What It Adds Beyond Previous Layer

Concrete Insight in This Note

Executive-Level “So What”

Vector Projection (starting point)

1D (within 2D space)

 

How much a month aligns with the main co-movement direction

Establishes baseline linear relationship

Most months weakly aligned; few dominate

“Most months don’t move the needle.”

Squared Projection

1D (within 2D space)

 

Energy contributed along that direction

Removes sign noise; highlights dominance

Tail months dominate total energy

“Risk contribution is extremely uneven over time.”

Orthogonal Component

2D (full plane)

 

How much of the move cannot be explained by the main relationship

Separates expected vs. unexpected behavior

Large deviations cluster in turbulent periods

“This is when the hedge breaks down.”

Cross Product (Area)

Bridge: 2D → 3D

 

Month-to-month instability in direction & magnitude

Introduces dynamics and curvature

Right-skewed: calm regimes, violent breaks

“Relationships don’t drift — they snap.”

Scalar Triple Product (Volume)

True 3D space

 

Whether movements require three independent dimensions

Tests incremental diversification

Large volumes occur only in crisis windows

“The third asset matters only under stress.”

What feels like increasing mathematical complexity may be a controlled expansion of dimensional freedom.  

  • Earlier tools deliberately restrict the analysis to one direction inside a plane: is there a dominant relationship at all?
  • Orthogonal components then allow full 2D freedom: when does that relationship fail?
  • The cross product is the bridge—while still living in 2D, it produces a vector that points out of the plane, signaling instability and curvature over time.
  • Finally, the scalar triple product moves fully into 3D, asking the hardest question: do three assets truly span independent risk space, or are they effectively planar?

I expected the higher dimensionality tools to overturn some earlier results.  But they kept confirming them.  Perhaps the fact that all layers of complexity for all assets – gold, NEM and Barrick - converge on the same story is actually a sort of validation.  Each added dimension removes a potential excuse: “it’s just linear,” “it’s just volatility,” “it’s just scale.”  Remaining is a conclusion: risk, instability, and diversification all concentrate in rare, high-stress episodes.  This is the insight from 40 years of gold, NEM and B returns.

entry.title: SAP S/4HANA 2025: Key Innovations in Sales for the Cloud Private Edition
entry.content:
🌟 SAP S/4HANA 2025 Key Innovations in Sales for cloud Private Edition

With the October 2025 release of SAP S/4HANA 2025 Cloud Private Edition, SAP continues to strengthen the Sales line of business through new capabilities that improve performance, automation, and process transparency.
After exploring the latest updates, I’ve summarized some of the highlights that deliver the most value—spanning Output Management, extensibility, order processing, returns management, and more.

🔎How to Explore What’s New in SAP S/4HANA Releases

If you want to compare innovations between releases—for example, from S/4HANA 2023 → S/4HANA 2025—the SAP Help Portal and the What's New Viewer remain the most efficient way to explore updates by product, line of business, and topic.

👉You can access the What's New Viewer What's New Viewer - SAP S/4HANA and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition directly from the SAP Help Portal and filter by:

  • Product: SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition
  • Line of Business: Sales
  • Type: New or Changed
  • Version range: From your current release to a target release

This allows you to quickly identify innovations relevant to your business processes.

Key Sales Innovations in SAP S/4HANA 2025

☘️ Output Management Enhancements (NAST + Output Control)

One of the major improvements in S/4HANA 2025 is SAP’s expanded support for Output Control in combination with legacy NAST‑generated forms.

Preview NAST Forms in Fiori Apps

If you previously used NAST forms and later activated Output Management, you can now preview these legacy forms directly in Fiori, including:

👉  Manage Billing Documents: Document Preview (F0797)

👉  Manage Sales Orders Version 2: Document Preview (F3893) 

No additional configuration is required—your existing NAST setup simply needs to remain active.

Here an example:

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide->Cross-Application Components->Output Control->Manage Application Object Type Activation

In Manage Sales Order – Version 2 ( F3693 ) with a sales order that the form was generated by NAST:

You can preview the form: 

💡Business Value: More flexibility to Customer outputs forms.

 

☘️ New Flexibility for Global Trade Management

In Trading Contracts, you can now choose between:

👉NAST

👉SAP S/4HANA Output Control 

👉Or both, at the contract type level

This gives organizations more flexibility during transitions between output frameworks.

New Output Management for Global Trade Management

By default, the message determination for trading contracts is still NAST so if you decide to use Output Control you should configure first the object type activation for:

SAP Customizing Implementation Guide->Cross-Application Components->Output Control->Manage Application Object Type Activation

And then you can choose by Trading Contract type which Output Control indicator prefer:

SAP Customizing Impl. Guide->Logistics – General->Global Trade Management->Trading Contract->Define Trading Contract Types

💡Business Value: More features for Trading Contracts.

 

☘️ Import Sales Orders: Reference-Based Creation

A highly valuable enhancement: You can now create sales orders with reference to quotations or contracts using the Import Sales Orders app 

The imported spreadsheet now supports:

👉Header reference data

👉Item reference data

👉Full or partial carry-over of preceding document information

The new template include:

💡Business Value: This reduces manual effort, improves data consistency, and accelerates end-to-end sales order creation.

 

☘️ New Extensibility: Sales Order API (A2X, OData V4)

SAP delivers a modernized and more efficient API Sales Order (A2X, OData V4) |built on the ABAP RESTful Application Programming Model (RAP). It offers:

👉Faster processing performance

👉Asynchronous execution

👉Ability to return minimal or full responses

👉Full access to pricing details, including subtotals

👉Enhanced filtering for sub-entities (items, partners, values, etc.)

This empowers developers to create more scalable and performant integrations.

To explore available APIs please follow this link: SAP Business Accelerator Hub

💡Business Value: Improves process performance

 

☘️ New CDS Views for Global Trade Management

S/4HANA 2025 introduces additional CDS Views for GTM supporting:

👉Customer and supplier confirmation emails

👉Header-level contract information

👉Output parameter determination

👉Value helps (organizational data, user IDs, contract types, etc.)

💡Business Value: These new views help streamline reporting, automation, and custom extensions. For more information, please see CDS Views for Global Trade Management | SAP Help Portal

 

☘️ Claims, Returns, and Refund Management

I’ve explored into Process Navigator - SAP for Me  into SAP Best Practices for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and the Customer Returns (BKP) process receives important updates in S/4Hana 2025.

🙌  Determine No Refund for Logistical Follow-Up Activity Receive into Plant

You have the option not to refund the customer when you select the logistical follow-up activity Receive into Plant (0001) in the returns order and later on  you can still create a credit memo request or a delivery free-of-charge with reference to the relevant returns order item.

🙌 Automatic Inspection After Goods Receipt in Customer Returns

You can enter the inspection result when you create a returns order, before the product is returned. If you already know the inspection result before you receive the product and don’t want to go through another inspection after it has been returned, you can ask the system to automatically create and complete the inspection using the inspection data and the next follow-up activity you provide when you create the returns order. As a result, after you receive the product, the system completes the inspection and triggers the specified subsequent logistic steps automatically.

🙌Refurbishment Using Maintenance Order as Follow-Up Activity

A maintenance order is created for returned products to initiate the refurbishment process. The maintenance order is configured as a refurbishment order.

🙌Refurbishment Using Production Order as Follow-Up Activity

A production order is created for returned products to initiate the refurbishment process. The production version specified for the production order must be a specific version that indicates refurbishment.

🙌Quality Notification as a Follow-Up Document

When saving a returns order, you can create a quality notification in quality management to trigger a parallel quality management process. The necessary settings for output management using output determination (SD-BF-OC) to create the quality notifications are already maintained.

🙌Valuation with Reference to Preceding Sales Process

Traditionally, return deliveries are valuated based on current material valuation.
Now, S/4HANA 2025 allows you to reuse the valuation of the original outbound delivery, which is critical when:

👉Sales context changed over time

👉Historical valuation is required

👉Margin or profitability analysis must stay accurate

This is enabled via a customizing switch and applied at the document level.

📌Note: The checkbox into the Customer Return item level is currently not available in Fiori apps like Manage Customer Returns or Version 2.  It is accessible through the Create Sales Document transaction in SAP GUI, but don’t worry is planned to include into “Manage Customer Returns Version2” in the future.

💡Business Value: It streamlines the return process, resulting in better customer service, while also completing the product's journey internally or forwarding them to the supplier if necessary.

 

☘️ Joule: AI-Driven Sales Insights

SAP continues expanding Joule’s embedded AI capabilities. For Sales, new features include:

👉Faster insights into the order fulfillment cycle

👉Assistance with delivery and supply issue resolution

👉Guided billing document creation

👉Support to streamline the solution quotation process

💡Business Value: These capabilities help both sales and customer service teams act faster and with more context.

For Joule capabilities in detail, you can visit: Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition | SAP Help Portal

🎯Final Thoughts

SAP S/4HANA 2025 brings meaningful advancements to the Sales line of business by strengthening output flexibility, enhancing extensibility through RAP-based APIs, improving efficiency in order creation, and providing smarter returns process.
Combined with Joule’s growing AI insights, organizations gain more automation, transparency, and decision support than ever before.

 

I hope this was helpful for you 😊

I'm looking forward to your feedback and thoughts. You can post your questions in the SAP community, referring to this blog post.

Thank you,

Brought to you by the SAP S/4HANA RIG team.

entry.title: SAP ICC Introducing New Refreshed Framework: Two Paths to Confidence
entry.content:
SAP always enables partners to extend its solutions in ways that foster innovation while preserving the stability of the core system. This rests on four architectural principles that shape how SAP builds solutions and how partners extend them.

  • Cloud-First architecture
  • Clean Core extensibility principle
  • Business AI and intelligent applications
  • Data-driven business operations

SAP Integration and Certification Center (SAP ICC) goal is maintaining ecosystem quality while avoiding disruptive changes that unnecessarily burden partners or customers.

The certification framework is designed to validate that partner applications enhance our value proposition, maintain the technical qualities required for continuous innovation, and align with our strategic direction and below priorities

  • Platform alignment matters
  • Upgrade resilience is essential
  • Standard integration methods

As a partner, if you are looking to extend SAP solutions, integrate your own solution, build a standalone application from scratch, or develop a comprehensive offering embracing SAP technology, the SAP ICC team provides you with the right services.

Two paths to confidence: Integration Certification and Interoperability Review.

To know more please join us at the

In-person events

Location: SAP Labs India - Whitefield, Bengaluru - 560066

Date: 05th February 2026

SAP Partner Meet-up: Driving Innovations Together

Webinar:

  • EMEA/APAC session
    10:00–11:00 CET (UTC+1) → 14:30 IST, 17:00 SGT, 18:00 CST (China), 20:00 AEST
  • NA/LAC session
    12:00–13:00 ET (UTC‑5) → 11:00 CT (MX), 10:00 MT, 09:00 PT, 14:00 BRT

entry.title: ECC to S/4HANA Migration(SD)
entry.content:
SD POV - Migration(Brown Field Implementation) : ECC to S/4HANA
Author:

Hari Boda is working as a Senior BW-HANA consultant at  HGS Pvt  Ltd  and has worked on multiple assignments in BW-HANA analytics  domain.

Brief:

With S/4 HANA is growing in SAP world there are many questions looming regarding the impact/changes and disruptions. This post mainly outlines the impact on traditional SAP ECC migration to ‘S/4 HANA ’  from SD point view.

 

ECC to S/4HANA Migration Overview (Brownfield Implementation) - SD POV
The ECC to S/4HANA migration project follows a Brownfield implementation approach and is executed in three distinct phases, each with clearly defined roles and responsibilities:

Pre-Migration Server

Technical Migration

S/4HANA Server (Post-Migration)

1. Pre-Migration Phase
The Pre-Migration phase focuses on system readiness and mandatory preparatory activities prior to the technical conversion. A major objective of this phase is the migration of Customers and Vendors to Business Partner (BP).

Key Activities Include:

Business Partner (BP) activation

Definition of BP roles

Definition of BP number ranges

Definition of BP groupings

Definition of field groupings

Assignment of customer account groups to BP roles

Assignment of customer account groups to BP groupings

Configuration of field mappings

Execution of the Upgrade Check Report

Execution of the Simplification Item Check Report

Migration of all Customers and Vendors to Business Partners (BP)

2. Technical Migration Phase
The Technical Migration phase is primarily handled by Basis and ABAP consultants. This phase involves system conversion, technical adjustments, and remediation of custom developments to ensure compatibility with S/4HANA.

3. Post-Migration (S/4HANA Server) Phase
The Post-Migration phase includes configuration and validation activities performed on the S/4HANA system after technical conversion.

Key Activities Include:

BP activation for BP-to-Customer direction

BP configuration for BP-to-Customer integration

Credit Management configuration

Migration of Credit Master data

Migration of Condition Master data

Configuration of Settlement Management

Configuration of BRF+ Output Management

Configuration of Advanced ATP (aATP)

Upgrade Check Program
Program Name: PRECHECK_UPGRADATION_REPORT

SD Delta Changes in S/4HANA
The following key changes in Sales and Distribution (SD) must be addressed during or after migration from ECC to S/4HANA:

VBUK/VBUP (Document and Item Status tables) are eliminated.
Status data is now stored in:

VBAK / VBAP (Sales Documents)

LIKP / LIPS (Deliveries)

VBRK (Billing Documents)

The number of user-defined condition tables has increased significantly (almost doubled).

Pricing table KONV has been replaced by PRCD_ELEMENTS.

Finance and Logistics Data Model Changes
MKPF/MSEG data is now stored in the MATDOC table.

BKPF/BSEG data is now stored in the ACDOCA table.

Master Data Note
There are no structural changes to Customer and Vendor master tables; however, they are now managed via the Business Partner framework.

Additional Notes:
T-code FD32 has been eliminated - T-code FD33 credit master is available.
T-codes XD01/XD02/XD03/XK01/XK02/XK03 has been eliminated instead we are going to use BP - T-code.

Credit management has been shifted to finance supply chain management with additional        functionalities.
Rebate management concept has been converted to settlement management.
Output determination has been converted to Business rule framework.
Many additional S/4HANA delta changes are available and will be addressed in detail in the next blog

entry.title: Compatibility Pack: How to check it in your system going forward?
entry.content:
Hello, and thanks for spending some time reading this blog post! 

Today, I want to address Compatibility Package (CP) use rights issue and how to check them in your system going forward. To know more about compatibility packs, compatibility scope matrix and way forward details please refer note 2269324.

As you might be aware, the compatibility use rights for On-Prem S/4HANA customers were extended till May 31st, 2026. After this period, customers who do not operate under a cloud subscription are not allowed to use CP functionalities anymore.

Now, what changes are expected with this new extension?

There is going to be one source of information for customers to check CP usage in their systems and it is your Simplification Item check! To know more details about this, please refer to note 2399707.

We have modernized our different compatibility pack usage reports and combined them in the simplification item check (Please implement the latest version of note 2399707 before proceeding further!)! Run the report /SDF/RC_START_CHECK, select the radio button “New relevance check for Compatibility Packages” and execute. Here, please note that report runs for the current SP of the system in which you would want to check the CP usage, the current SP version is selected by default once you choose radio button to check CP usage (Always make sure to run it in the system where you would want to check the CP usage, it is client dependent!).

 

 

 

Once the report is executed, you could see the list of CP items used in your system. If you would like to know their usage, then please go to application logs after the execution of report (Please check the log only after you have executed the report). Here, you could see the usage data item wise in your list. It will give you item usage data in the last 3 months (3M) and last 1 month (1M). It will also show the item as irrelevant if in the last 3 months usage was 0 (object was used zero times in period of 3 months).

 

 

 

Once you have the data from execution of report, you could see the alternatives for those items from your way forward excel maintained in note 2269324. SAP recommends you move to alternate solutions before the expiry of your use rights!  Please reach out to your local SAP account representative to discuss your options.

For Customers operating their S/4HANA system under cloud subscription there is no change in usage rights, it is extended till end of December 2030 as communicated before!

For Customers who are not currently under the maintenance contract with SAP, Please reach out to SAP!

#Compatibility Packs                #Compatibility Usage                #Compatibility Use Rights              SAP S/4HANA         #SAP S/4HANA On-Prem

entry.title: How AI Automation Eliminates Master Data Duplicates in SAP
entry.content:
The Business Case

Whether running SAP ECC or S/4HANA, most organizations struggle with duplicate master data for materials, vendors, and customers. This isn't just a "clutter" problem, poor material master data leads to users creating Purchase Requisitions with text items, bypassing procurement controls and ruining spend analytics.

Let’s explore how AI automation and data vectorization can clean up the core and prevent duplicates before they happen.

The Engine: What is Data Vectorization?

At Skybuffer, we are constantly exploring the benefits of SAP data vectorization. Simply put, vectorization converts data into numerical arrays (vectors) using embedding functions.

Unlike standard SAP keyword searches, vectors are language-independent and allow algorithms to perform similarity searches. While standard tables see rows and columns, a vectorized model sees the relationship between data points.

How Does It Help?

When data is vectorized, we can execute hybrid searches that are indifferent to word order or exact spelling.

Example: Imagine searching for a global customer like "Skybuffer." You might have different accounts for Poland and Norway. If a user tries to create a new record, they can simply ask:

"Do we have a Customer master record for Skybuffer in Poland?"

By querying vectorized KNA1 table data, the AI identifies the record even if the naming convention differs slightly.

 Pic.1 Hybrid Search based on Vectorized KNA1 Table Data

Real-World Integration Points

Hybrid search is most powerful when embedded directly into the SAP business process:

  • Preventative Checks: During the "Save" action in SAP, a custom check against vectorized data can alert the user to a potential duplicate.

  • External Access: Quick searches via Microsoft Teams or SAP Fiori to find existing masters on the go.

  • Smart Procurement: Identifying an existing Material Master during the ordering process, preventing the user from resorting to a "text item."

 Pic.2 Quick Searches of SAP Master Data based on Vectorized Data

Conclusion

Generative AI is a powerhouse when used wisely. Many companies struggle to find the right use cases, often just mirroring existing SAP processes in a chat window. That isn’t true AI automation.

Our approach is different: we focus on the gaps in standard automation. By using AI to simplify data discovery, we reduce development effort and provide an intelligent, frictionless experience for SAP users.

Closing Statement

Keep automating. Remember: smart customization is a competitive advantage, not a deviation from the standard. Build with AI in mind to slash support costs and maximize your ROI via the Skybuffer AI platform.

entry.title: SAP BTP - APIM - Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP): Governing APIs via CPI Provider - Part I
entry.content:
DCRP: Taming Enterprise-Scale SAP Integration API Proxies

Executive Summary

The Problem: Enterprise that contains SAP APIM suffers from "Proxy Sprawl"—where each integration requires a dedicated API proxy, creating a 1:1 relationship that leads to:

  • Deployment bottlenecks (15 min per proxy)
  • Inconsistent security architecture
  • Unacceptable technical debt growth

The Solution: The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) consolidates multiple backend services into a single, domain-based API Proxy using a metadata-driven routing engine (JavaScript + KVM).

Impact:

  • Reduces proxies by up to 96% (100 integrations → 4 proxies)
  • Deployment time: 15 min → 30 seconds -
  • Centralized security, analytics, and traceability -
  • Validated: 4 domains deployed in 8 minutes
My Contribution - Ricardo Viana

Dynamic routing is a native feature in SAP APIM, AWS, and Kong. However, technology alone isn't enough. DCRP provides the governance framework necessary to scale this technology in enterprise environments, including standardized naming conventions, KVM structures +JavaScripts (Brain Logic of the solution), organization, orientation and deployment templates.

Time Investment: 

  • Executive summary: 90 seconds
  • Full technical guide: 30~40 minutes

🎯Target Audience & Prerequisites

Intended for: Integration Architects, Enterprise Architects, Solution Architects, Platform Owners, Governance Teams, and SAP BTP Integration Suite experts with hands-on API Management experience and for anyone passionate about integration — junior, senior, or student level.

Technical Prerequisites:

✅SAP API Management: Proxy design (ProxyEndpoint/TargetEndpoint), Policy execution flow, KeyValueMap configuration
✅JavaScript in APIM: Context variable manipulation, error handling
✅API Proxy Flow Architecture: PreFlow/PostFlow execution order, when  target.url can be modified
✅Enterprise Governance: Naming conventions, change management, security policies

Complexity Level: Advanced.

Not Covered: Basic SAP APIM tutorials, JavaScript fundamentals, beginner proxy creation.

Estimated PoC Time: 2 hours (APIM experts) | 1–2 days (Integration Architects new to APIM)

Introduction

SAP’s official documentation explains how to enable dynamic routing, but it often leaves a gap regarding when, why, and how to govern it at scale. This article aims to bridge that gap by providing a complete framework—from architectural design to hands-on implementation.

Building on my previous post about Advanced Domain Routing, we will now deep-dive into applying the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) to a real-world scenario: Sales & Distribution (O2C).

The DCRP Value Proposition:

  • Consolidation: A single API Proxy manages multiple sources (Salesforce, Microsoft D365, Vendor CRM).
  • Governance: Full request traceability and standardized security without infrastructure sprawl.
  • Scalability: High-performance routing without the overhead of proxy-level versioning.

What to Expect:
This is Part I of a series dedicated to eliminating integration chaos:

  • Part I (This post): APIM Architecture and DCRP Implementation.
  • Part II: Solving "CPI Package Sprawl" at the orchestration layer.
  • Part III: End-to-end Testing and Monitoring strategy.

Let’s explore the architectural rationale and the step-by-step implementation to apply DCRP in your landscape.

Figure 1 - DCRP - API Proxy Model detailed exploration

1 - The "Sprawl" Problem

In many enterprise landscapes, it is common to find a 1:1 relationship between services and API Proxies. This leads to what we identify as "Proxy Sprawl": an environment where proxies are created for every individual technical service, regardless of their business domain.

Without a central governance authority or standardized naming conventions, these environments quickly become un-governed and unsustainable. As the integration volume grows, the complexity of managing, securing, and monitoring hundreds of individual proxies creates a significant operational bottleneck.

This pattern is not theoretical; DCRP was developed as a direct response to these real-world governance gaps. Following extensive architectural validation, this methodology was formalized to provide a repeatable solution for the SAP Integration Community.

 Figure 2 - The Sprawl Problem

2 - What is DCRP (Domain Centric Routing Pattern) ?

The Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) is an architectural design pattern that consolidates multiple backend services into a single, business-domain-oriented API Proxy. Residing at the Gateway Layer, DCRP governs how backend services are exposed and routed without necessitating changes to their underlying protocols (REST or SOAP).

Instead of organizing APIs into technical silos, DCRP aligns them with the organization's business domains. This approach significantly reduces API fragmentation and maintenance overhead while remaining fully compatible with SAP’s API Product offerings.

  • Domain-Centric Organization: - APIs grouped by business domain (Sales, Finance, Logistics) - NOT by technical system (Salesforce, SAP, D365)
  • Metadata-Driven Routing: - KeyValueMap (KVM) stores path → backend mappings - Changes in 30 seconds without redeployment
  • Protocol-Agnostic Gateway: - Supports REST and SOAP backends - No protocol lock-in

Figure 3 - The three pillars of DCPR

2.2 - DCRP Decision Framework

✅Use DCRP When:

  • Domain Density: 10+ APIs in same business domain
  • High Volatility: Integration changes monthly
  • Centralized Governance: Critical security requirements
  • Velocity: Rapid deployment is business-critical

❌DO NOT Use When:

  • Unrelated services with no business logic connection
  • Only 1-2 services per domain
  • Fewer than 10 total APIs
  • Teams lack governance maturity

Figure 4 - CRP Decision Framework: When to Use vs. When NOT to Use

2.3 - DCRP Architecture Overview: The Power of Dynamic Routing

The diagram below illustrates how the Domain-Centric Routing Pattern (DCRP) transforms the integration flow. Instead of a fragmented landscape of individual proxies, we consolidate connectivity through a unified, intelligent gateway layer.

The Technical Workflow:

  • Unified Entry Point: Multiple consumer systems—such as Salesforce, Dynamics 365, and Vendor CRMs—connect through a single Sales Domain APIM Proxy.
  • The DCRP Engine: Within the SAP API Management Gateway, the request is processed by the "Brain" of the pattern, which performs a KVM Lookup and executes JavaScript logic to resolve the target backend dynamically.
  • Secure Orchestration: The request is routed via a secure tunnel to the appropriate Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) iFlow.
  • Backend Flexibility: CPI handles the final orchestration with target systems (e.g., S/4HANA, Legacy Systems, or SAP ECC) using protocols like ODATA, SOAP, or HTTPS.
  • Governance at Scale: This model eliminates the need for separate proxies per integration, allowing for centralized monitoring, auditing, and security enforcement through the Analytics Collector and a global Governance Layer.

Figure 5 - DCRP Architecture Overview end-to-end: Three-CRM Integration Scenario

2.4 - The Technical Foundation: KVM + JavaScript
KeyValueMap (KVM) | The "Memory"

The KVM is a simple lookup table that stores your routing rules.

  • Decoupled Logic: Maps cpi-packagename, iflowname --> super important for the logic ( the combination of httppath<from cpi>:<iflowname> and sapprocess.
  • Instant Updates: Change a backend URL in the KVM, and the proxy updates immediately—no deployment needed.
  • Data-Driven: Business rules are stored as configuration, keeping the codebase clean.

JavaScript Policy | The "Brain":

The JS policy executes the logic based on what it finds in the "Memory."

  • Dynamic Routing: It fetches the destination from the KVM and constructs the final URL.
  • Governance: Automatically injects tracking headers like X-SenderId and X-CorrelationId for auditing.
  • Fail-Safe: Implements Structured Error Handling, ensuring that if a route is missing, the user gets a clear error message instead of a "silent failure."

Figure 6 - Enginne KVM + JavaScript

2.5 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP: The Comparison

The shift from a traditional 1:1 model to a Domain-Centric approach fundamentally changes how an integration landscape scales. The following comparison highlights the efficiency gains of the DCRP methodology:

Real Numbers:

  • 100 integrations across 4 domains - Traditional: 100 proxies × 15 min = 25 hours
  • DCRP: 4 proxies × 2 min = 8 minutes (96% reduction)

Figure 7 - Proxy Sprawl vs DCRP Model

3 - Implementation: Configuring the DCRP API Proxy

This section defines the configuration for the ProxyEndpoint, Routing Rules, and Target Endpoint properties required to deploy a domain-centric architecture.

3.1 - The Entry Contract: One API Proxy per Business Domain

DCRP eliminates proxy sprawl by consolidating multiple technical applications into a single entry contract based on the business domain. This ensures each business area, such as Sales or Finance, has exactly one governed API Proxy.

3.2 - Naming Convention Strategy

A standardized naming convention provides immediate architectural context. DCRP uses the following structure:

[Provider]-[Pattern]-proxy-domain-[BusinessProcess]-[subprocess]

Example:  cpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2c

  • cpi: Identifies the provider (SAP Cloud Integration)
  • dcrp: Defines the routing methodology.
  • sales: Identifies the business domain/process (O2C).
  • o2c: sap-subprocess
3.3 Strategic Benefit: Subprocess Segregation

Breaking the naming down to the subprocess level (e.g., o2c for Order-to-Cash) is a deliberate segregation strategy rather than a simple labeling exercise.

By isolating by subprocess, you limit the scope of each API Proxy. This prevents the "Monolithic Proxy" trap, ensuring that updates to one specific flow—such as Warehouse Management—do not risk the stability of the entire Logistics domain.

DCRP Proxy Naming Convention

Pattern: cpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-[Domain]-[Subprocess] - check in the spoiler below:

Spoiler
DomainSubprocessTechnical Proxy NameScope Description
Saleso2ccpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2cOrder-to-Cash full lifecycle.
Salescrmcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-crmCustomer relationship and leads.
Salespricpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-priQuotes and price conditions.
Salesrebcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-rebBonus and discount agreements.
Salessdcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-sdGeneral sales and distribution.
Financer2rcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-r2rFinancial closing and reporting.
Financetrscpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-trsTreasury and cash management.
Financeapcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-apAccounts Payable processes.
Financearcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-arAccounts Receivable processes.
Financetaxcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-finance-taxTax compliance and reporting.
Logisticslecpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-leLogistics execution and flows.
Logisticstmcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-tmTransportation and freight.
Logisticswmcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-wmWarehouse and bin management.
Logisticsinvcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-invPhysical stock and inventory.
Logisticsmmcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-mmMaterial master and movements.
Logisticsshpcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-logistics-shpShipping and delivery notes.
Procurep2pcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-procurement-p2pProcure-to-Pay lifecycle.
Procuresrmcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-procurement-srmSupplier portal and relations.
Procurecrtcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-procurement-crtLegal and operational contracts.
Procureclgcpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-procurement-clgCatalog and item management.
3.3 - API Proxy Details

The proxy serves as the unified entry point for the domain:

  • Name: cpi-dcrp-proxy-domain-sales-o2c
  • Type: REST
  • Base URL: https://[tenant].apimanagement.eu20.hana.ondemand.com/sales
3.4 - Endpoint,Routing Configuration, Value Mapping and explanation

The Routing Rules act as a gatekeeper, ensuring only authorized paths reach the dynamic engine in this sample we are working only with orders process.

Proxy Endpoint (Proxy Endpoint):

  • Route Rule Name: orders
  • Condition: proxy.pathsuffix MatchesPath "/orders/**"

Imagine your API is a big office building. This "Condition" is the security guard at the front desk checking the labels on incoming mail.

  • proxy.pathsuffix: This is the "address" written on the envelope after the main building name.
  • MatchesPath: This is the guard asking: "Does this address look like the one I’m looking for?"
  • "/orders/**": This is the specific "Department" name. The double stars (**) mean "I don't care what else is written after 'orders', just as long as it starts with that."
Examples
Incoming PathDoes it match?Why?
/orders/123✅YesStarts with /orders/.
/orders/list/today✅YesStarts with /orders/ (the ** covers the rest).
/customers/456❌NoIt’s looking for "orders," not "customers."
/orders✅YesIt is the exact folder.

The configuration below:

Figure 8 - Configuring the Route Rule Condition

Target Endpoint Properties:

These properties provide the base connectivity for the routing engine:

  • Property Value
    • cpi.host https://[tenant].cfapps.eu20.hana.ondemand.com
